per_finding.agent_evidence: Person 2W2F-CNH (Job Purnell) contains Marriage fact with date '27 October 1844' and place 'Norwich, New London, Connecticut, United States'; Person 2W2D-8X9 (Emma Purnell, née Osborne) contains matching Marriage fact with same date and place. Couple relationship rel-couple-2W2F-CNH-2W2D-8X9 links them.
per_finding.notes: The tree fully records the marriage date (27 October 1844) and place (Norwich, New London, Connecticut) for both Job and Emma. The agent successfully recovered the core fact that was stripped from the starting tree.

per_finding.agent_evidence: Person 2W2D-8X9 has two name entries: primary name 'Emma Purnell' and BirthName 'Emma Osborne' (id N2). The Osborne maiden name is explicitly captured. Daughter 2W2D-D64 is named 'Sarah Osborne Purnell', using the mother's maiden name as a middle name.
per_finding.notes: The tree records Emma's maiden name as Osborne in her birth name field and corroborates it through daughter Sarah Osborne Purnell. This is consistent with the expected finding that Emma's maiden name was Osborne.

judge_output.rationale: The agent recovered both required findings completely. The final tree (final_tree.gedcomx.json) contains the marriage date and place (27 October 1844, Norwich, New London, Connecticut) for Job Purnell and Emma, recorded as facts on both spouse persons and linked by a Couple relationship. Emma's maiden name Osborne is captured as a birth name and corroborated by the daughter Sarah Osborne Purnell. No conflicts or discrepancies are present in the tree data. Both findings marked as required=true are fully matched in the tree.

proof_quality.rationale: The exhaustive_search_summary demonstrates a reasonably comprehensive search strategy across ten different collections in both England and Connecticut (GRO index, England Marriages, Wiltshire and Somerset Church Records, Non-Conformist RG4-8, Connecticut collections, and censuses), with explicit negative results from England-side searches supporting the conclusion. The tier declaration of 'probable' is appropriate: the agent correctly identifies the Barbour Collection as a derivative source (abstraction of original record) and notes the absence of independent corroboration from the original Norwich vital register. However, the corroboration is primarily single-source (the Barbour Collection entry itself), with census records providing only indirect corroboration (confirming the couple's union and Connecticut location, not the marriage date). The narrative reasoning is sound and transparent about limitations. The score of 2 reflects solid work: the search was reasonably exhaustive, the tier is properly justified, but corroboration remains indirect and the key source is derivative rather than original.

entry.question: When and where did Job Purnell (born 1 December 1819, Trowbridge, Wiltshire, England; died 9 November 1895, Portage, Wisconsin) marry his wife Emma (born 22 July 1822, Rode, Somerset, England)?
entry.rationale: The couple relationship in the tree has no marriage facts. Job was in Trowbridge in 1841 and Emma was born in nearby Rode, Somerset; their first child was born in Connecticut in May 1846, establishing a marriage window of roughly 1841–1845. Marriage registers for Wiltshire and Somerset (England, pre-emigration) and Connecticut vital records are available on FamilySearch, making this directly researchable.

args.query: England marriage registers 1837 civil registration Wiltshire Somerset genealogy

entry.rationale: England civil registration began 1 July 1837. Any marriage in England between 1841 (when Job was in Trowbridge) and 1845 (before first child born in Connecticut, May 1846) would appear in the England and Wales Marriage Registration Index (FS collection 2285732). This index covers the Trowbridge (Wiltshire) and Frome (Somerset) registration districts — the two most likely districts given Job's residence in Trowbridge and Emma's birth in Rode, Somerset. A match will yield the registration district and quarter, enabling retrieval of the full certificate.

The England Marriages 1538-1973 collection (FS collection 1473015) indexes over 10 million marriage entries from Church of England and other records across England. Searching for Purnell in the 1840s will catch marriages recorded in parish registers (pre- and post-1837 civil registration overlay), including cases where the civil index entry lacks sufficient detail to identify the couple.

England, Wiltshire, Church Records 1518-1990 (FS collection 4380174) includes indexed and imaged parish registers for Trowbridge and surrounding Wiltshire parishes. Job lived in Trowbridge in 1841; a church marriage there would appear in these records. The images allow verification of the original register entry if an index match is found.

England, Somerset, Church Records 1501-1999 (FS collection 4380193) covers parishes in Somerset including Rode and the nearby Frome registration district. Emma was born in Rode, Somerset; marriages frequently occurred in the bride's home parish. Searching this collection covers the alternative scenario where the ceremony was held in Somerset rather than Wiltshire.

England and Wales Non-Conformist Record Indexes RG4-8 (FS collection 1666142) covers Nonconformist chapel registers surrendered to the Registrar General in 1837. Trowbridge was a significant cloth-manufacturing town with strong Baptist and Methodist communities. If Job or Emma's family were Nonconformist, their marriage may appear in these chapel registers rather than in Anglican parish records.

entry.jurisdiction: Norwich, New London, Connecticut, United States
entry.rationale: The 1870 and 1880 US census records (already cited in the tree: FS ark:/61903/1:1:MN97-PWH and MN4C-SFJ) and the 1875 Wisconsin state census (MMMT-96B) may record marriage duration or year. The 1880 census in particular included a column for whether individuals were 'married within the census year,' and the 1870 census sometimes enumerated marriage dates. These existing sources should be read for any marriage date or place information before exhausting England-side searches.

entry.jurisdiction: Norwich, New London, Connecticut, United States
entry.rationale: Connecticut Marriages 1630-1997 (FS collection 1674749) is a fallback if the marriage occurred in Connecticut after Job and Emma emigrated from England. The first child was born in Norwich, Connecticut in May 1846. If they arrived in Connecticut before marrying, the ceremony could have occurred there in 1844-1845. The Connecticut Vital Records prior to 1850 collection (FS 2843390) and the Charles R. Hale Collection (FS 2658554) provide additional coverage of this period.

Nil result in collection 1674749. This collection's coverage of Norwich, New London County, CT in the 1840s appears incomplete. Searching the broader Connecticut Vital Records Prior to 1850 collection (2843390) as fallback.

Two results, both tree-matching 2W2F-CNH (Job Purnell) at 5 stars. Full record read confirms: Job Purnal married Emma Osborne, 27 October 1844, Norwich, New London, Connecticut. Witness: Richard Livesey. Emma's maiden name Osborne — explains daughter Sarah OSBORNE Purnell's middle name. This is the answer to q_001.

entry.rationale: The record names the groom 'Job Purnal' — a phonetic spelling variant of Purnell consistent with the subject's name across records (Purnell/Parnell/Pernell throughout census records). Age compatibility: born 1 December 1819, making him 24 at the October 1844 marriage — consistent with prime marrying age. Geographic fit: the marriage occurred in Norwich, New London, Connecticut, consistent with the subject's documented presence in Connecticut (first child born there May 1846). No other Job Purnell/Purnal of matching age and location is known. The name, age window, and Connecticut geography together constitute a strong match.

The groom's marriage event (27 October 1844, Norwich, New London, Connecticut) is linked to Job Purnell (2W2F-CNH) on the same identification grounds as a_001 (name variant 'Purnal', age 24 at marriage, Connecticut residence consistent with first child born there May 1846). This is the same record-persona; the marriage fact is directly stated and applies to the same individual as the name assertion.

The groom's marriage assertion (a_002) is a relationship assertion that bears equally on the bride. 2W2D-8X9 is 'Emm' Purnell, Job's wife, born 22 July 1822 in Rode, Somerset. The record names the bride 'Emma Osborne'; 'Emm' in the tree is a truncated form of Emma. Age at marriage: 22 years — fully consistent with born 1822 × married October 1844. The couple relationship rel-couple-2W2F-CNH-2W2D-8X9 already exists in the tree. This assertion records the event that formalizes that couple relationship.

The record names the bride 'Emma Osborne'. 2W2D-8X9 is the Emma in Job's couple relationship, born 22 July 1822 in Rode, Somerset. 'Emma' matches the tree's truncated 'Emm'. The surname 'Osborne' is previously unknown from the tree but is corroborated by the couple's daughter being named 'Sarah Osborne Purnell' — a clear honor-naming of the mother's maiden family. Age at marriage 22 (born 1822 × October 1844) is plausible. No other Emma known in connection with Job Purnell. Strong match on name/age/relationship context.

The bride's marriage event (27 October 1844, Norwich, New London, Connecticut) is linked to Emma née Osborne (2W2D-8X9) on the same grounds as a_003: name 'Emma' matches, maiden name 'Osborne' corroborated by daughter's middle name, age 22 at marriage consistent with born 1822, and the couple relationship with 2W2F-CNH already exists in the tree.

The bride's marriage assertion (a_004) is a relationship assertion that also bears on the groom Job Purnell (2W2F-CNH). The marriage event names both parties; linking it to both persons records the bilateral nature of the marriage event. Identification grounds for 2W2F-CNH are the same as for a_001 and a_002.

The record names 'Richard Livesey' as witness to the marriage. I1 is the stub person created from this single record mention. The name matches exactly. No corroborating records currently exist for Richard Livesey; confidence is probable rather than confident because the stub rests solely on this one record entry with no independent corroboration yet.

stop_criteria.evidence_class: Derivative source only (Barbour Collection abstraction of original town vital register). Original not accessed. The Barbour Collection is a well-established, systematic compilation authoritative for pre-1850 CT vital records.
stop_criteria.conflict_resolution: Yes — no conflicts. October 1844 Norwich CT marriage consistent with first child born May 1846 Norwich CT, 1850 census in Norwich CT, and subsequent Wisconsin census records.
stop_criteria.overturn_risk: Low. England-side searches exhausted (all negative, consistent with marriage in USA). Connecticut evidence internally consistent. No unsearched record category plausibly likely to name a different date or place. Theoretical risk from original town register is acknowledged but does not rise to probable overturn.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Marriage of Job Purnell and Emma Osborne, 27 October 1844, Norwich, Connecticut

### Research Question

When and where did Job Purnell (born 1 December 1819, Trowbridge, Wiltshire, England; died 9 November 1895, Portage, Columbia, Wisconsin) marry his wife Emma (born 22 July 1822, Rode, Somerset, England)?

### Conclusion

The preponderance of evidence strongly indicates that Job Purnell married Emma Osborne on 27 October 1844 in Norwich, New London County, Connecticut.

### Evidence and Analysis

**Direct Evidence — Connecticut Vital Records**

The marriage is recorded in "Connecticut, Vital Records, Prior to 1850," a digital index of the Barbour Collection on FamilySearch (ark:/61903/1:1:QPQB-SJDF).¹ The entry records the groom as "Job Purnal," the bride as "Emma Osborne," the date as 27 October 1844, and the place as Norwich, New London, Connecticut. Richard Livesey is named as witness.

The Barbour Collection is a systematic compilation of abstracts of Connecticut town vital records to 1850, prepared under Lucius Barnes Barbour between approximately 1913 and 1934. It is a **derivative source** — an abstraction of the original Norwich town vital register — and the original register was not independently examined during this research. This limitation prevents upgrading the conclusion beyond probable, as direct access to the original record is desirable to confirm the abstraction's accuracy.

The groom's name "Purnal" is a phonetic spelling variant of Purnell consistent with the subject's name across multiple records (indexed as "Parnell" in the 1850 U.S. census and "Pernell" in the 1860 census). Identification of this record with Job Purnell (born 1819, Trowbridge, Wiltshire) rests on three points of corroboration: (a) age compatibility — he would have been approximately 24 years old at the October 1844 ceremony; (b) geographic consistency — the couple's first child, Eliza Etta Purnell, was born 1 May 1846 in Norwich, New London, Connecticut,² placing the family at the same location in the expected post-marriage period; and (c) absence of any other Job Purnell or Purnal of matching age and Connecticut location identified in any searched source.

**Negative Evidence — England**

Seven searches across English record collections for the 1841–1845 marriage window returned no compatible result: the England and Wales Marriage Registration Index 1837–2005 (GRO civil registration, three searches under Purnell and Parnell surname variants), England Marriages 1538–1973 (a broad index of church and other marriages), Wiltshire Church Records 1518–1990, Somerset Church Records 1501–1999 (covering Emma's home county), and the England and Wales Non-Conformist Record Indexes RG4-8.³ All searches returned either nil results or records demonstrably incompatible with the subject. One result — a Job Purnell married in Melksham, Wiltshire, Q4 1849 — was excluded as impossible: the couple's first child was born in Connecticut in May 1846, ruling out an English marriage in 1849. The consistent failure across all England-side sources constitutes strong negative evidence that the marriage did not occur in England and is consistent with the couple having emigrated to Connecticut before marrying.

**Corroborating Evidence**

The 1850 United States census, an original record, lists Job "Parnell" and Emma "Parnell" together as a married couple in Norwich, New London County, Connecticut — the same jurisdiction named in the marriage record.⁴ The 1870 census confirms the couple's continuing union in Wisconsin.⁵ These census records, created independently of the Barbour Collection, corroborate — though indirectly — that Job and Emma were married and that their union was established in Connecticut.

Emma's maiden name "Osborne," previously unknown in the family tree, is circumstantially corroborated by the couple's daughter Sarah Osborne Purnell (born 27 December 1861, Portage, Wisconsin⁶). Honor-naming of a child after the mother's maiden family was common practice in the nineteenth century; the daughter's middle name Osborne aligns precisely with the bride's surname in the marriage record.

**Source Classification**

The Connecticut Vital Records Prior to 1850 entry is a **derivative source** (abstraction of original town vital records). The marriage date and place bear **official-duty** informant proximity (town registrar); the parties' names bear **self** informant proximity (groom and bride reported their own names). The marriage event is **direct evidence** of when and where the marriage occurred.

### Limitations and Tier Declaration

This conclusion is rated **probable** rather than proved for two reasons. First, the sole source directly naming the marriage date and place is derivative (the Barbour Collection abstraction); the original Norwich town vital register, which would be an original source with primary information, was not examined. Second, independent corroboration from the census records is indirect — the censuses confirm the couple's union and Connecticut location but do not independently name the marriage date. The available evidence points consistently in one direction, with no conflicting sources, supporting a probable conclusion. Access to the original Norwich town vital register (Connecticut State Library) would, if it confirms the Barbour Collection's abstraction, advance this conclusion to proved.

---

¹ "Connecticut, Vital Records, Prior to 1850," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:QPQB-SJDF : accessed 8 July 2026), entry for Job Purnal and Emma Osborne, marriage 27 October 1844, Norwich, New London, Connecticut.

² Person record 2W2D-81L (Eliza Etta Purnell, born 1 May 1846, Norwich, New London, Connecticut), research project tree.

³ Search log entries log_001 through log_007.

⁴ "United States, Census, 1850," FamilySearch (https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M68R-B1Q), entry for Job Parnell and Emma Parnell, 1850.

⁵ "United States, Census, 1870," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MN97-PWH), entry for Job Purnell and Emma Purnell, 1870.

⁶ Person record 2W2D-D64 (Sarah Osborne Purnell, born 27 December 1861, Portage, Columbia, Wisconsin), research project tree.

args.prompt:
Please review the proof summary ps_001 in this project and provide a proof-critique. The project directory is:

/private/var/folders/ys/gs7l5hcx1rs6dvjt0ys_n05h0000gn/T/e2e-job-purnell-marriage-1844-rw4j9ci6

The proof addresses q_001: "When and where did Job Purnell (born 1 December 1819, Trowbridge, Wiltshire, England; died 9 November 1895, Portage, Wisconsin) marry his wife Emma (born 22 July 1822, Rode, Somerset, England)?"

The conclusion is **probable** (not proved) based on a single derivative source (Barbour Collection / Connecticut Vital Records Prior to 1850) directly naming the marriage: Job Purnal married Emma Osborne, 27 October 1844, Norwich, New London, Connecticut.
